subject: Mathematics
text: If a quantity y varies with another quantity x, satisfying some rule y = f (x), then {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} represents the rate of change of y with respect to x.
text_html: If a quantity y varies with another quantity x, satisfying some rule y = f (x), then {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) or {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} represents the rate of change of y with respect to x.

subject: Mathematics
text: If two variables x and y are varying with respect to another variable t, i.e., if x = f (t) and y = g (t), then by chain rule {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}{/tex} , if {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}} \neq 0{/tex} .
text_html: If two variables x and y are varying with respect to another variable t, i.e., if x = f (t) and y = g (t), then by chain rule {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}{/tex}, if {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}} \neq 0{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f is said to be increasing on an interval (a, b) if x 1 < x 2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) {tex}\leq{/tex} f(x 2 ) for all x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).
text_html:
A function f is said to be increasing on an interval (a, b)
if x1 < x2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) {tex}\leq{/tex} f(x2) for all x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f is said to be decreasing on (a, b) if x 1 < x2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) {tex}\geq{/tex} f(x 2 ) for all x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).
text_html:
A function f is said to be decreasing on (a, b)
if x1 < x2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) {tex}\geq{/tex} f(x2) for all x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f is said to be strictly increasing on an interval (a, b) if x 1 < x 2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) < f(x 2 ) for all x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).
text_html:
A function f is said to be strictly increasing on an interval (a, b)
if x1 < x2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) < f(x2) for all x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f is said to be strictly decreasing on (a, b) if x 1 < x 2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x 1 ) > f(x 2 ) for all x 1 , x 2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).
text_html:
A function f is said to be strictly decreasing on (a, b)
if x1 < x2 in (a, b) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x1) > f(x2) for all x1, x2 {tex}\in{/tex} (a, b).

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f(x) is said to be increasing in (a, b) if {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.
text_html: A function f(x) is said to be increasing in (a, b) if {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) {tex}\geq{/tex} 0.

subject: Mathematics
text: A function f(x) is said to be decreasing in (a, b) if {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0.
text_html: A function f(x) is said to be decreasing in (a, b) if {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) {tex}\leq{/tex} 0.

text_html: Increasing and Decreasing Functions:

subject: Mathematics
text: A point c in the domain of a function f at which either f′(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable is called a critical point of f.
text_html: A point c in the domain of a function f at which either f′(c) = 0 or f is not differentiable is called a critical point of f.

subject: Mathematics
text: f is said to have a maximum value in I, if there exists a point c in I such that f (c) {tex}\geq{/tex} f(x) for all x {tex}\in{/tex} I. The number f(c) is called the maximum value of f in I and the point c is called a point of maximum value of f in I.
text_html:
f is said to have a maximum value in I, if there exists a point c in I such that f (c)  {tex}\geq{/tex}f(x)  for all x {tex}\in{/tex} I.
The number f(c) is called the maximum value of f in I and the point c is called a point of maximum value of f in I.

subject: Mathematics
text: f is said to have a minimum value in I, if there exists a point c in I such that f (c) {tex}\leq{/tex} f (x), for all x {tex}\in{/tex} I. The number f (c), in this case, is called the minimum value of f in I and the point c, in this case, is called a point of minimum value of f in I.
text_html: f is said to have a minimum value in I, if there exists a point c in I such that f (c) {tex}\leq{/tex} f (x), for all x {tex}\in{/tex} I. The number f (c), in this case, is called the minimum value of f in I and the point c, in this case, is called a point of minimum value of f in I.

subject: Mathematics
text: f is said to have an extreme value in I if there exists a point c in I such that f (c) is either a maximum value or a minimum value of f in I. The number f (c), in this case, is called an extreme value of f in I and the point c is called an extreme point.
text_html: f is said to have an extreme value in I if there exists a point c in I such that f (c) is either a maximum value or a minimum value of f in I. The number f (c), in this case, is called an extreme value of f in I and the point c is called an extreme point.

subject: Mathematics
text: A monotonic function f in an interval I, we mean that f is either increasing in I or decreasing in I.
text_html: A monotonic function f in an interval I, we mean that f is either increasing in I or decreasing in I.

subject: Mathematics
text: Every continuous function on a closed interval has a maximum and a minimum value.
text_html: Every continuous function on a closed interval has a maximum and a minimum value.

subject: Mathematics
text: First Derivative Test: Let f be a function defined on an open interval I. Let f be continuous at a critical point c in I. Then If {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) changes sign from positive to negative as x increases through c, then c is a point of local maxima. If {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) changes sign from negative to positive as x increases through c, then c is a point of local minima. If {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) does not change sign as x increases through c, then c is neither a point of local maxima nor a point of local minima. In fact, such a point is called point of inflection.
text_html:
First Derivative Test: Let f be a function defined on an open interval I. Let f be continuous at a critical point c in I. Then

  1. If {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) changes sign from positive to negative as x increases through c, then c is a point of local maxima.
  2. If {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) changes sign from negative to positive as x increases through c, then c is a point of local minima.
  3. If {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) does not change sign as x increases through c, then c is neither a point of local maxima nor a point of local minima. In fact, such a point is called point of inflection.

subject: Mathematics
text: Second Derivative Test: Let f be a function defined on an interval I and c {tex}\in{/tex} I. Let f be twice differentiable at c. Then x = c is a point of local maxima if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex} (c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex} (c) < 0 The values f(c) is local maximum value of f. x = c is a point of local minima if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex} (c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex} (c) > 0 In this case, f(c) is local minimum value of f. The test fails if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex} (c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex} (c) = 0. In this case, we go back to the first derivative test and find whether c is a point of maxima, minima or a point of inflexion.
text_html:
Second Derivative Test: Let f be a function defined on an interval I and c {tex}\in{/tex} I. Let f be twice differentiable at c. Then

  1. x = c is a point of local maxima if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex}(c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex}(c) < 0 The values f(c) is local maximum value of f.
  2. x = c is a point of local minima if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex}(c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex}(c) > 0 In this case, f(c) is local minimum value of f.
  3. The test fails if {tex}f^{\prime} {/tex}(c) = 0 and {tex}f^{\prime \prime}{/tex}(c) = 0. In this case, we go back to the first derivative test and find whether c is a point of maxima, minima or a point of inflexion.

subject: Mathematics
text: ​​​​Working rule for finding absolute maxima and/or absolute minima Step 1: Find all critical points of f in the interval, i.e., find points x where either {tex}f^\prime{/tex} (x) = 0 or f is not differentiable. Step 2: Take the end points of the interval. Step 3: At all these points (listed in Step 1 and 2), calculate the values of f. Step 4: Identify the maximum and minimum values of f out of the values calculated in Step 3. This maximum value will be the absolute maximum value of f and the minimum value will be the absolute minimum value of f.
text_html:
​​​​Working rule for finding absolute maxima and/or absolute minima
Step 1: Find all critical points of f in the interval, i.e., find points x where either {tex}f^\prime{/tex}(x) = 0 or f is not differentiable.

Step 2: Take the end points of the interval.
Step 3: At all these points (listed in Step 1 and 2), calculate the values of f.
Step 4: Identify the maximum and minimum values of f out of the values calculated in Step 3. This maximum value will be the absolute maximum value of f and the minimum value will be the absolute minimum value of f.
